title: Unisys vs. Florida HRS — Expert Witness Memoir (Senate Chamber Tallahassee, c.1990-1991)
author: Peter S. Kastner (memoir); Perplexity AI (research context)
abstract: Peter S. Kastner recalls his expert-witness role in the Florida Department of Health and Rehabilitative Services (HRS) bid protest — Unisys vs. IBM/EDS — in the early 1990s. Kastner went through a box of computer paper to find the single IBM performance estimate sheet that proved the proposed mainframe plus DB2 architecture could not support the required terminal counts and average response time, and testified in the Florida Senate Chamber in Tallahassee. Although Unisys lost the bid, the IBM/EDS implementation failed catastrophically — a $200 million fiasco involving system crashes during food stamp distribution, national records for error rates, and EDS suing for $46 million in unpaid bills. The fallout helped motivate Congress to pass two pieces of Senator William Cohen's IT-procurement reform legislation — the Information Technology Management Reform Act and the Federal Acquisition Reform Act (jointly the Clinger-Cohen Act of 1996) — which remain in force today. era: 1990s; mood: reflective; trigger: Perplexity research session January 2026 on government IT procurement disasters
importance_rationale: The memoir documents Kastner's pivotal expert-witness role in a procurement disaster that directly motivated landmark federal IT-procurement reform legislation (Clinger-Cohen Act 1996). It is the first-person companion to the existing litigation summary in psk-litigate98-72601c, adding specific testimony details not found elsewhere in the archive.
relevance_rationale: Government IT procurement architectural lessons — centralized vs. distributed architecture, vendor lock-in, independent technical evaluation requirements — remain front-and-center. The Clinger-Cohen Act still governs federal IT acquisitions in 2026.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 1990-era testimony correctly predicted the DB2-on-mainframe response-time collapse that subsequently materialized (initially measured in minutes; peak CPU 95-100%). His broader argument — that centralized mainframe architecture would fail for geographically distributed interactive systems — foreshadowed the entire 1990s mainframe-to-distributed transition that played out exactly as he warned across dozens of government IT projects.
